name: DFS
coreIntuition: DFS explores as deep as possible along each branch before backtracking. But the real power of DFS isn't just traversal — it's the **information you can gather during the recursion**. Entry/exit times, subtree sizes, back edges (cycles), articulation points — all of this comes from understanding what happens as you go deeper AND as you return. DFS is a framework for computing things about graph structure.
mentalModel: Imagine exploring a maze by always taking the first unexplored turn and going as far as possible. When you hit a dead end, you backtrack to the last junction and try the next turn. The key insight: when your recursive call returns, you have complete information about everything reachable from that branch. This 'post-order' information is incredibly powerful — subtree sizes, presence of cycles, farthest distances.
whenToUse: You need to explore all nodes or find if a path exists
The problem involves connected components or regions
You need to detect cycles in a graph
The problem requires processing subtrees (tree DP, subtree queries)
You need topological ordering (DFS with finish-time ordering)
You need to classify edges (tree/back/forward/cross edges)

thinkingFramework.step: 1. Define what each recursive call represents
thinkingFramework.detail: DFS(node) should answer: 'process node and everything reachable from it'. Be clear about what 'process' means for your problem — is it marking visited? Computing a value? Counting something?

thinkingFramework.step: 2. Decide what information flows DOWN (parameters) vs UP (return values)
thinkingFramework.detail: Parameters carry information from parent to child (depth, current path). Return values carry information from children back to parent (subtree size, found target). This split is crucial for tree/graph DP.
For undirected graphs: mark visited before recursing. For directed graphs: you may need three states (unvisited, in-progress, completed) to detect cycles — a back edge goes to an in-progress node.

thinkingFramework.step: 4. Process in pre-order, post-order, or both
thinkingFramework.detail: Pre-order (before recursing on children): useful for assigning entry times, passing information down. Post-order (after all children return): useful for aggregating subtree information up.

commonTraps.trap: Stack overflow on deep graphs with recursive DFS
commonTraps.howToAvoid: For very deep graphs (10^5+ nodes in a chain), use iterative DFS with an explicit stack. Recursive DFS is cleaner but has stack depth limits.

commonTraps.trap: Not distinguishing between 'visited' and 'in current path' for cycle detection
commonTraps.howToAvoid: In directed graphs, use 3 colors: white (unvisited), gray (in current DFS path), black (fully processed). A back edge to a gray node means cycle. A edge to a black node is fine.
Loop over all nodes, starting DFS from each unvisited node. A single DFS call only explores one connected component.

interviewVariantStrategy: DFS variants center on what you compute during traversal: (1) **Cycle detection** — track nodes in current path (gray nodes). (2) **Topological sort** — reverse post-order of DFS. (3) **Articulation points/bridges** — track discovery times and lowest reachable ancestor. (4) **Tree DP** — return values from children aggregate information (e.g., diameter = max over children of height1 + height2). (5) **Flood fill** — DFS on grid to find connected regions. The key: DFS is a framework. The algorithm doesn't change — what changes is what you compute in the pre-order and post-order steps.

resources.title: William Fiset — DFS Algorithm
resources.why: Excellent visual walkthrough of DFS with applications to cycle detection and topological sorting

resources.title: CP-Algorithms — DFS and Applications
resources.why: Covers DFS edge classification, cycle detection, topological sort, and connected components

resources.title: MIT 6.006 — DFS, Edge Classification, Cycle Detection
resources.why: MIT lecture that explains the theoretical foundations — entry/exit times and edge classification
